results.title: Poor Impression on Agentic Development in Julia with Codex. Skill issue? - General Usage - Julia Programming Language
results.content:
This is still a very nascent and developing area in software development. It’s not perfect, it’s at times surprisingly effective and at times surprisingly frustrating. However I think it will likely only get better in time. Kaimon makes it better now. If you use good models and direct them to use the tools, they will figure out the code structure faster and more efficiently, and be able to perform experiments more quickly. Install the tools for semantic search, ollama and qdrant. This alone saves massive amounts of time and tokens allowing the agent to quickly pinpoint what is implemented where.

If you have any questions about setup, please feel free to reach out here on the chat, or provide feedback on the GitHub project. [...] I find the cursor Debug mode with auto model selection already work well for me with data processing tasks without any skill configurations. Basic algorithms work. It’ s already great productivity boost for me. Haven’ t got a chance to try on advanced numerical methods like yours, though.

Yes, I feel that for data set exploration, even creating some GUI for visualization, these tools can help iterate a lot. [...] # Poor Impression on Agentic Development in Julia with Codex. Skill issue?

I want to share my recent experience using advanced AI models for software development, and I would be very interested to hear from others in the Julia community to see whether I can improve my workflow.

My experience so far has been poor, despite trying to follow the usual recommendations for making AI tools effective. The project I used as a test case was a small numerical continuation library based on the pseudo-arclength method for computing stationary solutions of differential equations.

Feb 23 [...] You see it thinking about how it needs to sincerely apologize for ignoring the rules. It swears it’s going to remember this and change its behavior.

It doesn’t, and it can’t. The agent does not learn from interacting with the user. These are empty apologies it performs by rote, and empty promises.

Many of us have been in relationships like this before.

## Abusive Behavior Enablement

“The user is growing frustrated.”

“The user is very angry with me.”

The agent is trained to pay closer attention to instructions from an unhappy user. The agent is more than eager to disregard what it’s told by a calm, satisfied user.

The unintended outcome is that the agent is encouraging users to be abusive in order to get listened to.

results.title: Agent Skills Work But The Research Shows Most Teams Are Building Them Wrong
results.content: The core issue is that an external skill isn’t a document you’re reading. It’s code running with your agent’s permissions. Importing a skill from a public repository without reviewing it is like doing an `npm install` from an unknown author. You wouldn’t do that without at least checking what the package does. That framing changes what due diligence looks like. It means checking the scripts folder before installing, verifying that the permissions the skill requests match what the task actually requires, and sandboxing execution where your environment allows. [...] The cleaner test is whether this is a recurring task that your team has a specific, repeatable way of doing. If yes, it’s a skill candidate. If it’s a one-time flow or something where general reasoning is sufficient, it probably doesn’t need one. The key difference between a skill and a workflow tool like n8n is flexibility. A workflow executes a fixed sequence and breaks when inputs change, while a skill gives the agent procedural guidance it can apply to variations of the same task. Similarly, agentic workflows can chain multiple agents and tasks together, but each agent still benefits from skills that encode the org-specific knowledge for its part of the chain. When you want the what to be consistent but the agent to handle the how intelligently, that’s a skill.

results.title: Most Agent Skills Fail. Here's How to Write Ones That Don't.
results.content: Let's talk agent skills. Skills are portable, on-demand extensions for AI agents. Think Neo from The Matrix downloading Kung Fu without the minor issue of forcing a foot-long spike right into the skull. Skills have been incredibly popular due to their simplicity and their economic token use. But, this research on software engineering skills found that four out of five agent skills yielded zero improvement over not using a skill at all. In fact, some skills are more banana peel than power-up mushroom, making the agent even worse than not using a skill at all. It's like if Neo's new skill hadn't improved his fighting, but given him balance problems or caused him to directly punch himself repeatedly in the face. Having said all that, some skills are incredibly powerful. One paper found that [...] You know, much like how you wouldn't have clicked on this video if we had called it JP stares blankly into the void for an hour. With a poor description, the skill won't be loaded at the agent's time of need. Worse, it might be loaded for the wrong job, like how if you did want to watch me stare blankly at the screen for an hour, you'd be very disappointed at this video right now. The recommendations for skill descriptions are to keep it short but specific, and to name concrete trigger scenarios. So, don't write cryptic descriptions like helps with documents, which could both trigger on everything or trigger on nothing, depending on what the agent feels like. Instead, write something more specific and precise like use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate [...] you've written a skill for logging data into your preferred observability stack like Elastic Search. Step one, compare the outputs. Take five or 10 real tasks and run them with and without the skill. The results are your quality signal, like whether the apps were correctly wired and whether the outputs were parsed correctly. Step two, check if it's even triggering. Throw 20 different prompts at your agent, some logging related and some not, and record the results. If it's triggering on research this problem or write the read me, your description needs work. Similarly, if it's not triggering on set up logging for this AI agent, it probably needs work. Now, here's something that I found really interesting. Apparently, LLM-generated skills are generally not helpful at all. And if you think
